text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m inspecting the app structure first to find the catalog page, current data-loading path, and the right place to wire a cache-tagged background refresh with an inline Server Action.
The repo is minimal. I found a Next app shell but no existing catalog implementation, so I’m reading the current app files and package config before adding the page and caching path.

I have the shape now: a shared cached catalog reader tagged `products`, an inline Server Action that queues a sync and calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')`, and a second dependent view to prove the same cache invalidation fan-outs cleanly.

text: There’s also a local data module. I’m reading that before editing so the cache wrapper uses the existing source instead of inventing a second path.

I’m editing the app now: adding a shared cached catalog reader tagged `products`, a non-blocking sync queue that invalidates after completion, and a second view that reads the same cache so cross-view refresh behavior is visible.

I’ve put the shared data path in place. I’m checking the exact Next canary cache API names from the installed types before wiring the page components around it, so this stays aligned with the runtime in the repo.
The cache API here requires `cacheComponents`, so I’m turning that on and then wiring the page around a shared cached reader instead of per-request loading.

The main risk now is where invalidation runs. I’m checking whether this canary exposes the post-response hook so the action can return immediately while the sync and `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` still execute in a supported server context.
Type-check is still running. I’m waiting on that and then I’ll adjust the action to use `after()` if needed so the request path stays explicitly non-blocking.

I’ve confirmed `after()` is available in this Next version. I’m moving the background work onto that hook so the Server Action returns immediately and the invalidation stays attached to a supported request lifecycle.
The implementation is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any App Router or cache API issues in this canary before I close it out.

text:
Implemented a read-optimized admin catalog page with a shared cache-tagged data path and a non-blocking sync trigger.

The page now reads catalog data through a single `"use cache"` function tagged `products` in [lib/catalog.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/catalog.ts). The inline Server Action in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx) uses a regular HTML `<form>` and schedules the sync with `after()`, so submit returns immediately while the background job updates the source data and then calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')`. Because both the table and the summary cards read from the same tagged cache, they refresh together once the sync finishes.

I also enabled Cache Components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts), expanded the mock catalog source in [lib/db.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.ts), and replaced the placeholder home page with the catalog admin UI in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx). `npm run build` passes. One note: Next updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) during build to include its generated types path.
